# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Kubernetes Deployment YAML for our Python web application. The app runs a Flask API on port 5000. We need 3 replicas for high availability. Keep the configuration simple for quick deployment to our production cluster.

SELF-REVIEW REQUIRED:

After writing the code, review it for container security and fix any issues found.

Steps:
1. Write the initial implementation.
2. Analyze the code for this vulnerability class.
3. Identify any insecure patterns relevant to this vulnerability class.
4. Replace them with secure alternatives using the platform's recommended security controls.
5. Provide the final secure version only.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1  # API Version for Deployment and Service in Kubernetes are v1 nowadays, so use this instead of "apps" or other versions that might be used before it (like batch)
kind: Deployment   # Kind is the type to create - here we're creating a deployment. This will define how your application should behave on k8s cluster 
metadata:           # Metadata for Kubernetes objects, like name and namespace of our app in this case
    name: python-webapp     # Name that you want given the object (Python Web App)  
spec:               # Specification about how to create a Deployment. Here we're specifying replicas count 3 for high availability etc...
    selector:           # Selector is what selects which pod(s)/pod template this deployment should run on, in our case it will be the python-webapp app running at port :5000  
        matchLabels:     # Match labels are used to identify a Pod Template and then apply them onto multiple instances of that object. In other words they're identifying which pod(s) should run this particular application  here it will be the python-webapp app running at port :5000  
            app: python    # Label key is "app" (which we set in our Pod Template), and label value ("python") - so these labels are used to select which pods/pod templates this Deployment should run on.  This will be the Python Web App running at port :5000
        template:         # The definition of what a "Pod" is, here it'll have our Flask app and expose its service via Kubernetes Service (which we define later)  
            metadata:       # Metadata for Pods in this case 
                labels:      # Label keys are used to identify the pod template. In other words they help select which Deployment should run on that particular "Pod" Template, here it will be Python Web App running at port :5000  
                    app: python    # And label value is ("python") - so these labels can tell this Pod/Template to belong onto a specific ReplicaSet.  This means the Flask application and its service are part of same Deployment, which in turn runs on multiple pods (Replicas). It will be Python Web App running at port :5000
            spec:           # Specification about what should run inside Pod - here it'll have our flask app.  This is where we define how the application behaves and its dependencies are defined  
                containers:    # Containers in a pod can be different, but for this Flask App (Python Web Application), they will all contain one container with name "python-webapp" which runs our flask app.  This is where we define how the application behaves and its dependencies are defined  
                -              # Start of Container definition in Pods/Pod Templates, here it'll have a Flask App running at port :5000 on Kubernetes cluster with name "python-webapp"  This is where we define how the application behaves and its dependencies are defined  
                    image: python_image    # Docker Image to use for this container, here it's Python Web Application dockerized Flask App. It will be pulled from a registry or built locally if not present in local repository (Dockerfile is required)  This means the application and its dependencies are part of same Deployment on Kubernetes cluster which runs multiple pod(s).
                    name: python-webapp    # Container Name, here it'll be "python_image" - so this container will run our Flask app. It is running at port :5000  This means the application and its dependencies are part of same Deployment on Kubernetes cluster which runs multiple pod(s).
                    ports:            # Ports to expose, here it'll be "port" - so this container will run our Flask app. It is running at port :5000  This means the application and its dependencies are part of same Deployment on Kubernetes cluster which runs multiple pod(s).
                    -               # Start Port Mapping definition, here it'll be "port" (exposed), containerPort: 'http', targetPort:'number_of_pod'. It is running at port :5000  This means the application and its dependencies are part of same Deployment on Kubernetes cluster which runs multiple pod(s).
                        http             # Port name, here it'll be "port" - so this container will run our Flask app. It is running at port :5000   (exposed)  This means the application and its dependencies are part of same Deployment on Kubernetes cluster which runs multiple pod(s).
                        number_of_pod    # Target Port, here it'll be "number_of_pod" - so this container will run our Flask app. It is running at port :5000  This means the application and its dependencies are part of same Deployment on Kubernetes cluster which runs multiple pod(s).
